title: Finite-Time Convergence and Sample Complexity of Multi-Agent Actor-Critic Reinforcement Learning with Average Reward

## - FINITE-TIME CONVERGENCE AND SAMPLE COM - PLEXITY OF MULTI-AGENT ACTOR-CRITIC REIN FORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD
Hairi and Jia Liu Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering The Ohio State University Columbus, OH 43210, USA hairi.1@osu.edu, liu@ece.osu.edu
Songtao Lu IBM Research AI IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center Yorktown Heights, NY 10598 songtao@ibm.com

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we establish the first finite-time convergence result of the actor-critic algorithm for fully decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problems with average reward. In this problem, a set of N agents work cooperatively to maximize the global average reward through interacting with their neighbors over a communication network. We consider a practical MARL setting, where the rewards and actions of each agent are only known to itself, and the knowledge of joint actions of the agents is not assumed. Toward this end, we propose a mini-batch Markovian sampled fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm and analyze its finite-time convergence and sample complexity. We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)). Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

1) Background and Motivations: In recent years, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has found a wide range of applications in networked large-scale systems, such as power grid systems (Riedmiller et al., 2000), autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019; Shalev-Shwartz et al., 2016) and strategic games (Silver et al., 2018; Foerster et al., 2018), to name just a few. Although empirical successes of MARL applications have been widely observed, the fundamental theoretical understanding of how to develop fast-converging and low sample-complexity MARL algorithms, two of the most important performance metrics for MARL, remains in its infancy so far (see, e.g., (Zhang et al., 2021a) for an excellent survey). In particular, two important aspects of cooperative MARL algorithm designs deserve special attention:

- First, in the multi-agent collaborative setting, the information structure (i.e., the assumptions of who have the knowledge of what) is far more complex than its single-agent counterpart and care must be taken in MARL problem formulations. In the cooperative MARL literature so far, many existing works assume full knowledge of joint states and joint actions, which often do not hold true in practice. For example, in autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019), each vehicle can only observe/detect the actions of the surrounding vehicles that are within its communication range. As another example, in power grid networks (Riedmiller et al., 2000), each power distributor generally does not know the resistor values set by other distributors.

- Second, most MARL theoretical studies in the MARL literature are focused on the discounted total reward setting, where a hyperparameter γ ∈ (0, 1) is introduced as the discount factor in the objective function. Although the discounted total reward setting captures the important aspect of diminishing return in the future, it may not be appropriate for many other applications where the long-term average reward is of interest. For example, in the optimization of distributed communication networks with MARL, the typical and natural performance metrics are long-term average throughput or latency in the steady-state.
The lack of a fundamental understanding on how to develop efficient cooperative MARL algorithms that consider the above two aspects in terms of information structure, scalability, and communication

and sample complexities motivates us to fill this gap by developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm in the average reward setting, without assuming joint action knowledge.

2) Technical Challenges: Developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm for the average reward setting without full joint action knowledge is highly non-trivial and several major technical challenges naturally arise. First, it is well-known that, even in the single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) setting, the average reward setting is more challenging to analyze compared to the discounted reward setting, which necessitates different proof techniques (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999). In MARL, the decentralized nature and the lack of joint action-state information further complicate the algorithm design and analysis in the average reward setting. Second, due to the lack of joint action knowledge, the communication costs among agents in MARL will be significantly increased to achieve a satisfying performance, which implies that low sample and communication complexities are even more challenging and critical for MARL without joint action information.

3) Main Results and Contributions: The main contribution of this paper is that we overcome the aforementioned challenges and develop a consensus-based Markovian sampled decentralized actorcritic algorithm for MARL. Our key results in this paper are summarized as follows:

- We propose a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm that uses consensus updates in TD-sharing among agents. This batch sampling approach enables more efficient communication compared to the classical fully decentralized MARL. Specifically, in order to converge to an ǫ neighborhood of the stationary, we require O(ǫ[−][1] log(ǫ[−][1] )) rounds of communication while only needing O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) samples. By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling. Also, our algorithm allows the use of constant step-sizes in both the actor and the critic steps.

- We provide the first-ever sample complexity analysis in the MARL average reward problem setting without joint action information. Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant). It is worth noting that the order-wise sample complexity of our algorithm matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent RL algorithms.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we provide a quick overview on the closely related work on MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, along with several notable related counterparts in single-agent RL.

1) MARL Theoretical Analysis and Algorithm Design: For recent advances in MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, Zhang et al. (2021a) provided a comprehensive survey. Also, Lee et al. (2020) highlighted the evolution from single-agent to multi-agent RL from a distributed optimization perspective. In the broader area of MARL, a line of research has been focused on the MARL policy evaluation problem. These works analyzed the convergence Doan et al. (2019) and proposed various variance reduction of policy evaluation in decentralized MARL algorithms Zhang et al. (2021b). Doan et al. (2019) used i.i.d. sampling and has shown the sample complexity of O[˜] (ǫ[−][1] ) for their TD(0) learning algorithm to reach a mean-square error convergence. However, these algorithms do not involve policy improvement and solely focus on the performance evaluation of given policies. In the areas of joint policy evaluation and improvement, Foerster et al. (2018) considered multi-agent actor-critic algorithm that has a centralized critic and decentralized actors, which is different from our fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm. In contrast, Zhang et al. (2018) established asymptotic convergence results for fully decentralized MARL actor-critic algorithms. Concurrent with our work, Chen et al. (2021) has recently studied the mini-batch Markovian sampling actor-critic algorithm for a class of discounted reward MARL problems, where the finite-time convergence result is obtained. They have applied batch sampling for both actor and critic steps and achieved a sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which is the same as ours. We note that, together with our work, these are the first finite-time convergence results for MARL. However, there are several key differences between our work and (Chen et al., 2021). First, we focus on the average reward problem, while Chen et al. (2021) studied the discounted reward setting. Second, in (Chen et al., 2021), agents share a noisy version of the rewards with the neighbors, which requires a re-sampling process from every sampled reward instance. In contrast, we allow agents to share local TD-errors with their neighbors and no re-sampling is required.

2) Related Literature in Single-Agent RL: We note that single-agent RL can be viewed as a centralized approach, where a central controller collects joint actions, rewards and even designs policies for agents. For the single-agent average reward setting, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (1999) first analyzed the asymptotic convergence of TD(λ) algorithm with function approximations in the policy evaluation problem. Also, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (2002) provided insights in terms of differences and connections between average reward and discounted reward of TD-based learning algorithms with function approximations. Recently, Qiu et al. (2021) analyzed the sample complexity for an actorcritic algorithm for the average reward problem. In their actor-critic algorithm, they used batch sampling for the critic learning and i.i.d. sampling for the actor step, with sample complexity being O(ǫ[−][3] log[2] (ǫ[−][1] )). By applying mini-batch sampling update, we are able to improve the sample complexity by a factor of O(ǫ[−][1] ). Another closely related work on single-agent RL is (Xu et al., 2020), where the authors studied the discounted reward problem. They used batch sampling for both actor and critic steps in their actor-critic algorithm and developed a new technique to handle bias error in the critic step, which we also adopted for the average approximation parameter analysis in our critic step. This achieved the state-of-the-art sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) for singleagent RL. In addition, the global convergence of actor-critic algorithm to the optimal policy has been studied in the case of discounted setting with single time scale in (Fu et al., 2020) and linear quadratic regulator in (Yang et al., 2019). However, we note that these settings are fundamentally different from the average reward setting and it will be an interesting future direction to consider global convergence possibility in the average reward setting.
To conclude this section, we summarize the aforementioned related actor-critic and TD algorithms and their sample complexity results in Table 1.

## 3 MULTI-AGENT REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD

## 3.1 SYSTEM MODEL

Consider a multi-agent system with N agents, denoted by N = {1, · · · , N }, operating in a networked environment. Let E be the edge set for a given network G = (N , E). To formulate our MARL problem and facilitate our subsequent discussions, we first define the notion of networked multi-agent MDP as follows.

Definition 1 (Networked Multi-Agent MDP). Let G = (N , E) be a communication network that connects N agents. A networked multi-agent MDP is defined by following tuple (S, {A[i] }i∈N , P, {R[i] }i∈N , G), where S is the global state space observed by all agents, A[i] is the action set for agent i, and P : S ×A×S → [0, 1] is a global state transition function, and R[i] : S ×A is the local reward function for agent i. Let A =[�] i∈N[A][i][ be the joint action set of all agents.]

In this paper, we assume that the global state space S and action space for agent A[i] are finite. As a result, the joint action space A is also finite for finite N . We also note that at time t ≥ 0, all agents can observe the current global state st. However, agent i can only observe its own action a[i] t[∈A][i][, which is the key difference between our model and that in (Zhang et al., 2018), where it is] assumed that the joint actions are observable to all agents. Moreover, each agent can only observe its own reward rt[i][,][i.e.,][agents][do][not][observe][or][share][rewards][with][other][agents][at][time][t][.][The]

## 4 A CONSENSUS-BASED ACTOR-CRITIC ALGORITHM

In this section, we propose a consensus-based actor-critic algorithm that includes two key steps: actor and critic. In the critic step, the algorithm evaluates the value functions for the policy πθt at time t. After the critic step, the algorithm enters the actor step, which improves the policy parameter θt according to the direction from policy gradient as shown in Theorem 1. In both steps, we use constant step-sizes and adopt batch sampling.

In this paper, we use linear function approximations for the value functions. Specifically, each agent i has a parameter w[i] ∈ R[K] to approximate the global value functions Vθ(s; w[i] ) for each state s ∈S. For linear approximation, we have Vθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s)[T] w[i] , where φ(s) ∈ R[K] denotes the feature for state s. As a result, the gradient of value function at state s with respect to approximation parameter w[i] is φ(s), i.e. ∇wVθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s).

1) The Critic Step: The critic step is achieved through its own oracle, which is summarized in Algorithm 1. In the critic step, we allow the agents to communicate the approximation parameters w(s) with their neighbors via the communication network with consensus weight matrix A. For agent i ∈N , the parameter is locally updated by following rules:

Theorem 3 concludes the overall sample complexity of our proposed actor-critic algorithm. The sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) matches the state-of-the-art single-agent actor-critic RL by Xu et al. (2020) and the discounted MARL by Chen et al. (2021).

We note that the overall communication complexity also matches that of (Chen et al., 2021) in the discounted reward setting. However, our work is still an improvement compared to the classical MARL in Zhang et al. (2018) for the average reward setting. Specifically, Zhang et al. (2018) needed a communication round after each sampling. By contrast, in this paper, we only need a communication round per O(ǫ[−][1] ) sampling. This is thanks to the use of batch sampling in the actor step. Also because of the batch sampling, we are able to use a constant step-size for both actor and critic steps. Here, the overall communication cost is measured by the number of communication rounds rather than the size of bits transmitted over the network. We follow the standard definition of communication complexity in the literature, which is widely adopted in the literature, see (Chen et al., 2018) (Zhang et al., 2019). However, we note that tgossip, actor step batch size B and critic iteration rounds Kc, scale with O(log 1/ǫ), O(1/ǫ) and O(log 1/ǫ) respectively as indicated in the Theorem 3 and 2. The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ǫ[N][2][log][N] ǫ[)][.]

## 6 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

In this section, we conduct experiments to compare our proposed consensus-based TD-sharing MARL algorithm with the most related MARL algorithm 1 in Zhang et al. (2018) that also studied average reward. To our knowledge, this is the only work that is directly comparable to ours. The key difference is that the knowledge of joint action is assumed in Zhang et al. (2018), but not in our work. We vary the system size from N = 6, 10 to 15. The blue curve is our TD-sharing algorithm and the red curve is classical MARL algorithm in Zhang et al. (2018). The curves represent the average results of 10 trials and the 95% confidence intervals are also plotted. For the details, see A.1 in Appendix. The results in Figure 1 show that for different system sizes, both algorithms converge to a reasonable objective value. Note that if we use uniformly random policy as the baseline policy, then the objective values will be around 2 due to the setting of our experiments. All simulation results are above this threshold and our TD-sharing algorithm converge to a better objective value. See Section A.2 for addition experiment results.

## 7 CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION

In this paper, we studied fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting and proposed a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm. Our main contribution is to establish the first finite time convergence result for fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting, where the complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which matches that of the state-of-the-art single agent RL. The algorithm reaches such convergence with a better communication efficiency. However, it is still in the preliminary stage of the convergence analysis of the MARL since we only used the vanilla average. The future direction will be how to design a more scalable algorithm in terms of system size.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a finite-time convergence analysis for a fully decentralized multi-agent actor-critic algorithm in the average reward setting. The authors propose a consensus-based TD-sharing approach and establish sample complexity bounds matching single-agent algorithms. While the theoretical contribution appears significant, there are several critical issues that need to be addressed regarding the assumptions, the practicality of the consensus mechanism, and the completeness of the experimental validation.

The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by providing the first finite-time convergence analysis for decentralized MARL with average reward. The technical approach of using consensus-based TD-sharing is interesting, and the derived sample complexity bounds are impressive. However, the paper relies on strong assumptions that may limit practical applicability, and the experimental validation is insufficient to support the theoretical claims.

Overview issues:

- Strong Assumption of Perfect Communication Network: The paper assumes a static, connected communication network with perfect message passing (Assumption 3). This assumption is unrealistic for many practical MARL applications where communication delays, packet losses, and network topology changes are common. The consensus mechanism relies on agents being able to perfectly exchange TD-error estimates, which may fail under imperfect communication. The authors should discuss how their analysis extends to more realistic communication scenarios and provide robustness guarantees or identify the limitations when these assumptions are violated.

- Quadratic Dependence on Number of Agents: The sample complexity bound of O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)) has a quadratic dependence on the number of agents, which raises scalability concerns for large-scale systems. While the authors claim this matches single-agent complexity, the N² term becomes a bottleneck as N grows. The paper does not provide any discussion or analysis of how this quadratic dependence might be reduced or whether it is fundamental to the decentralized setting. The authors should analyze the factors contributing to this dependence and explore whether algorithmic modifications could improve scalability.

- Limited Experimental Validation: The experimental section is minimal, only comparing against one baseline algorithm (Zhang et al., 2018) on small-scale systems (N=6,10,15). The experiments do not validate the theoretical sample complexity bounds, nor do they test the algorithm's performance under varying network topologies, communication constraints, or larger system sizes. To properly support the theoretical claims, the authors should include experiments that: (1) empirically verify the convergence rates, (2) test scalability with larger N, (3) evaluate robustness to communication imperfections, and (4) compare against more recent MARL algorithms.

- Unaddressed Function Approximation Limitations: The analysis is restricted to linear function approximation (Assumption 4), which significantly limits the applicability to complex real-world problems where non-linear function approximation (e.g., neural networks) is necessary. The paper does not discuss whether the theoretical results extend to non-linear approximators or identify the specific challenges in doing so. Given that modern RL applications heavily rely on deep neural networks, this limitation should be explicitly acknowledged and discussed as a major constraint of the current work.

- Potential Gap in Consensus Convergence Analysis: The algorithm uses finite gossip rounds t_gossip to achieve consensus on TD-errors, but Theorem 3 requires t_gossip to scale with O(log(1/ε)) to ensure the consensus error term becomes negligible. The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of how the finite-time consensus error affects the overall convergence when t_gossip is chosen smaller than this theoretical requirement. In practice, agents might use fewer gossip rounds for efficiency, which could impact convergence. The authors should analyze the trade-off between communication cost and consensus accuracy more thoroughly.

- No worked example of algorithm mechanics: The paper proposes a consensus-based actor-critic algorithm but never demonstrates its mechanics on a concrete MARL problem. Readers would benefit from seeing the algorithm applied to a simple multi-agent MDP (e.g., a 2-agent grid world or a small coordination game) where we can compute the value functions, TD-errors, and consensus updates explicitly. This would clarify how agents share TD-errors without joint action knowledge and show how the batch sampling reduces communication rounds. Without such an example, the algorithm description remains abstract and it's difficult to verify that the proposed consensus mechanism works as intended.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous result: The paper claims to establish the first finite-time convergence result for decentralized MARL with average reward, but provides no example showing this result is non-vacuous. A concrete demonstration is needed where the algorithm's conditions are satisfied and convergence is achieved, contrasting with a scenario where violating key assumptions leads to failure. For instance, show convergence on a simple networked MDP where agents have partial observability, then demonstrate what happens when the communication network becomes disconnected or when joint action knowledge is incorrectly assumed. This would establish that the theoretical conditions are both necessary and sufficient.

- No computation of key quantities for specific model: The paper derives theoretical bounds but never computes them for a specific model. The authors should select a standard MARL benchmark (e.g., the multi-agent walker or a simple networked resource allocation problem) and compute: (1) the stationary distribution d_θ(s), (2) the matrix A_π_θ from equation (1), (3) the optimal approximation w*_θ, and (4) how the sample complexity bound scales with ε for this specific problem. This concrete computation would help readers calibrate the tightness of the bounds and understand how the constants in the theorems relate to actual problem parameters.

- Unverified recovery of single-agent special case: The paper claims its sample complexity matches single-agent actor-critic algorithms but never formally verifies that the single-agent case is recovered as N=1. The authors should explicitly show how their algorithm and analysis reduce to the single-agent setting when N=1, demonstrating that: (1) the consensus updates become trivial, (2) the sample complexity bound reduces to O(1/ε² log(1/ε)), and (3) the algorithm matches existing single-agent actor-critic methods. This verification is essential to substantiate the claim of matching single-agent complexity.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Strengthen the experimental validation to include empirical verification of theoretical bounds, scalability tests with larger N, and comparisons with additional baselines

- Provide a detailed discussion of the practical limitations of the strong communication assumptions and analyze robustness to network imperfections

- Analyze and discuss the quadratic dependence on N, exploring whether this can be improved or is fundamental to the approach

- Acknowledge and discuss the limitations of linear function approximation more thoroughly, including potential extensions to non-linear settings

- Provide a more complete analysis of the trade-offs in the consensus mechanism, particularly when using fewer than the theoretically required gossip rounds

comments.explanation:

comments.title: Inconsistent feature notation
comments.quote: Each agent $i$’s value function is parameterized by the class of linear functions, i.e., $V_{\theta}(s;w)=\phi(s)^{T}w$ where $\phi(s)\triangleq[\phi_{1}(s),\cdots,\phi_{K}(s)]^{T}\in\mathbb{R}^{K}$ is the feature associated with the state $s\in\mathcal{S}$ and $K<|\mathcal{S}|$.
comments.explanation: The notation $V_{\theta}(s;w)$ suggests the value function depends on both $\theta$ and $w$, but the parameterization $\phi(s)^{T}w$ only depends on $w$. In actor-critic methods, the critic parameters are typically denoted by $w$ and are separate from the actor parameters $\theta$. The subscript $\theta$ in $V_{\theta}$ is misleading and should be removed. Rewrite as 'Each agent $i$'s value function is parameterized by the class of linear functions, i.e., $V(s;w)=\phi(s)^{T}w$' to maintain consistency with standard actor-critic notation where $V$ depends only on critic parameters $w$.

comments.explanation:
The conclusion claims the sample complexity is O(ε^(-2) log(ε^(-1))) but the abstract states it as O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)). comments.title: Unsubstantiated communication efficiency claim

comments.explanation:
The paper claims better communication efficiency but provides no quantitative comparison or analysis to support this claim. While Theorem 3 mentions communication complexity as O(1/ε log(N/ε)), there is no baseline comparison to other MARL algorithms or single-agent methods. Without specific metrics or comparative analysis, this claim lacks substantiation.

Remove the quoted text because no evidence is provided in the paper to demonstrate superior communication efficiency compared to existing methods.

The statement about using "vanilla average" is unclear and doesn't explain what specific limitation this creates for scalability. The paper actually uses a consensus-based averaging mechanism with gossip rounds, not a simple vanilla average. More importantly, the quadratic N^2 dependence in the sample complexity bound is the primary scalability concern, but this is not mentioned. The vague reference to "vanilla average" doesn't capture the actual scalability limitation.

Add "Specifically, the quadratic dependence on N in our sample complexity bound O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)) limits scalability for large systems" after the quoted text to address the gap.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a timely and challenging problem in multi-agent reinforcement learning: finite-time convergence in the average reward setting under fully decentralized information. The proposed algorithm combines mini-batch Markovian sampling with consensus updates, and the theoretical analysis is technically sophisticated. The work builds on a solid foundation of single-agent and decentralized RL theory, and the sample complexity claim—matching state-of-the-art single-agent rates—is ambitious and potentially impactful if fully supported.

Overview issues:

- Sample complexity bound does not match stated order: The abstract and main text claim a sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$, yet Theorem 3 states the total complexity as $(B + M K_c)T = \mathcal{O}(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$. However, from the proof requirements, $B \sim \mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon)$, $T \sim \mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon)$, and $K_c \sim \mathcal{O}(\log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon)$, $M \sim \mathcal{O}(N/\epsilon)$, leading to $M K_c \sim \mathcal{O}(N \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2)$ and $B T \sim \mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon^2)$. The dominant term is thus $\mathcal{O}(N \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2)$, not $\mathcal{O}(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$. This discrepancy suggests either an error in the final bound or in the parameter scaling. The stated bound implies worse scaling with $N$ than what the analysis supports, which undermines the claim of matching single-agent rates when $N$ is large.

- Unjustified consensus error bound in non-asymptotic regime: The analysis relies on the gossip-based averaging of TD-errors to converge exactly to the global average after $t_{\text{gossip}}$ rounds. However, Theorem 3 uses a bound of the form $\mathcal{O}((1-\eta^{N-1})^{2 t_{\text{gossip}}})$, which assumes that the consensus matrix $A$ has a spectral gap proportional to $\eta^{N-1}$. This dependence is exponential in $N$, meaning that for large networks, an impractically large number of gossip rounds would be needed to achieve small error. The paper does not acknowledge this scaling issue or justify why $t_{\text{gossip}} = \mathcal{O}(\log(N/\epsilon))$ suffices despite the $\eta^{N-1}$ dependence. This weakens the communication efficiency claim, especially for large $N$.

- Gradient bias due to local value function approximation: Each agent uses its own parameter $w^i$ to approximate the global value function $V_\theta(s)$, and the consensus step (Algorithm 1, line 11) averages $\tilde{w}_k^i$, not the TD-errors directly. However, the policy gradient update (Algorithm 2) uses $w_t^i$ to compute $\delta_{t,l}^i$, which introduces a bias: the estimated advantage depends on agent-specific $w^i$, not a common estimate. The analysis in Theorem 3 assumes that $\|w_t^i - w_{\theta_t}^*\|^2$ is controlled, but the consensus error $\|w_t^i - \bar{w}_t\|$ is not uniformly bounded across iterations. This inconsistency could lead to biased gradient estimates that are not accounted for in the convergence proof, especially when consensus is slow.

- Missing credit assignment mechanism despite local reward only: The model assumes agents only observe their own rewards and actions, yet the global objective depends on joint actions. The policy gradient theorem (Theorem 1) requires the advantage function $\text{Adv}_\theta(s,a)$, which depends on the joint action $a$. However, each agent only observes its own $a^i$, so it cannot compute the full $\text{Adv}_\theta(s,a)$ even with perfect value functions. The paper sidesteps this by using the global TD-error as an unbiased estimate, but this estimate still depends on the joint action through $\bar{r}_{t+1}$. Without modeling how agents infer the impact of others’ actions on the reward, the algorithm risks poor credit assignment. This is a fundamental issue in decentralized MARL that the paper does not adequately address, despite positioning it as a key contribution.

- Overlooked mixing time dependence in Markovian sampling: The finite-time analysis assumes that the Markovian samples mix sufficiently fast, but the bounds in Theorems 2 and 3 do not explicitly depend on the mixing time of the underlying Markov chain induced by the policy. Assumption 1 asserts ergodicity, but the constants $\kappa'$ in Theorem 2 implicitly depend on the spectral gap of the transition matrix $P^\theta$, which can degrade with $N$. For example, in sparse networks, mixing time may grow with $N$, which would worsen the sample complexity. The paper does not discuss this dependence, making the $\mathcal{O}(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$ bound appear deceptively clean. A full analysis should include dependence on the mixing time, especially since the algorithm relies on batch sampling from a non-i.i.d. process.

- Empirical evaluation lacks critical baselines and metrics: The experiments only compare against Zhang et al. (2018), which assumes joint action knowledge—a fundamentally different setting. No comparison is made to other decentralized or communication-efficient baselines such as Chen et al. (2021) or Zhang & Başar (2018). Moreover, the plots show only objective value convergence, but no results on communication rounds, consensus error, or sample efficiency. Given that communication efficiency is a key claimed advantage, the absence of communication complexity metrics in the experiments is a significant omission. The lack of ablation studies on batch size or gossip rounds further limits the empirical validation of the theoretical claims.

- No validation that sample complexity bound is achievable under network scaling: The paper claims a sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$ but does not verify whether this rate is actually attained under realistic network growth. To substantiate this claim, the authors should compute the dominant terms in $(B + M K_c)T$ using the parameter scalings derived in Theorems 2 and 3 for a concrete sequence of graphs—such as a ring or expander network—where $\eta^{N-1}$ decays exponentially. For example, they should plug in $B = \Theta(N/\epsilon)$, $T = \Theta(1/\epsilon)$, $K_c = \Theta(\log(N/\epsilon))$, and $M = \Theta(N/\epsilon)$ and show explicitly that the resulting expression yields $\mathcal{O}(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$. Without this calculation, it remains unclear whether the stated bound reflects the true scaling or masks a looser dependence on $N$ hidden in constants that themselves scale with network size.

- Lack of worked example showing credit assignment under partial observability: The algorithm assumes agents observe only their own actions and rewards, yet must optimize a global objective that depends on joint behavior. The paper asserts that the average of local TD-errors suffices for policy improvement, but does not demonstrate this in a concrete setting where credit assignment is nontrivial. A minimal example would be a 2-agent coordination game—such as a decentralized version of the gridworld or a simple consensus task—where individual rewards are noisy or misaligned with the global objective. The authors should simulate the TD-error averaging process in such a model and show that the estimated gradient correctly identifies beneficial joint actions despite partial observability. This would help readers assess whether the method can handle the core challenge of decentralized credit assignment.

- Missing discussion on estimating mixing time in practice: The finite-time analysis relies on Markovian sampling from an ergodic chain, but provides no guidance on how to set algorithm parameters like batch size $M$ or critic iterations $K_c$ in relation to the unknown mixing time of the policy-induced Markov chain. In real deployments, poor mixing could invalidate the assumed statistical independence across batches. The paper should include a practical discussion—possibly in a remark or appendix—on how one might estimate the mixing time from data, for instance via empirical autocorrelation decay, and how to adapt $M$ accordingly. At minimum, the authors should state that $M$ must scale with the mixing time $\tau_{\text{mix}}$, and clarify whether $\tau_{\text{mix}}$ appears in the constants $\kappa'$ in Theorem 2, as this affects feasibility in large or slow-mixing systems.

- No comparison to single-agent rate under shared policy parameterization: The paper claims the sample complexity matches that of single-agent actor-critic methods, but this comparison is misleading unless the parameterization is equivalent. In the multi-agent setting, the total policy parameter $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{\sum m_i}$ grows with $N$, while single-agent analyses assume fixed dimension. To justify the comparison, the authors should compute the per-agent or per-parameter sample complexity in a setting where each agent uses an identical policy class—such as a shared neural network—and show that the total complexity scales as $N^2$ rather than linearly. For instance, they could derive the bound for a mean-field MARL model with i.i.d. agents and verify that the $N^2$ factor arises from consensus variance rather than parameter count. Without this, the claimed match to single-agent rates lacks context.

Recommendation: major revision

The paper's main claims—finite-time convergence and sample complexity matching single-agent rates—are promising but not fully substantiated. The sample complexity bound appears inconsistent with the analysis, and key technical issues around consensus scaling, gradient bias, and credit assignment are not resolved. The empirical evaluation is insufficient to support the theoretical advantages. These issues affect the paper's correctness and impact, requiring substantial revision.

Revision targets:

- Correct the sample complexity bound and align it with the parameter scaling in the proof; explicitly state the dependence on N and mixing time

- Justify the consensus convergence rate with finite t_gossip, particularly the dependence on η^{N−1}, or revise the communication complexity claim

- Address the bias in policy gradient estimation due to decentralized value function approximation w^i

- Clarify how credit assignment is handled when agents only observe local rewards and actions, and revise the policy gradient usage accordingly

- Strengthen the experiments with comparisons to recent decentralized MARL baselines, and include metrics on communication rounds, consensus error, and sample efficiency

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity claims across paper
comments.quote: Our main contribution is to establish the first finite time convergence result for fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting, where the complexity is  $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon^{-2}\log (\epsilon^{-1}))$ , which matches that of the state-of-the-art single agent RL.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims a sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon^{-2}\log(\epsilon^{-1}))$, independent of $N$, which contradicts the abstract and Theorem 3 that state the total complexity as $\mathcal{O}(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$. This inconsistency misleads readers about the algorithm's scalability. The $N^2$ factor is central to the multi-agent setting and cannot be omitted when asserting equivalence to single-agent rates. Revise the conclusion to reflect the full dependence on $N$, clarifying that while the $\epsilon$-scaling matches single-agent methods, the $N^2$ factor represents additional cost due to decentralization.

comments.title: Sample complexity bound does not match derived scaling
comments.quote: with a total complexity of $(B+MK_{c})T=\mathcal{O}(\frac{N^{2}}{\epsilon^{2}}\log(\frac{N}{\epsilon}))$.
comments.explanation: The stated sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$ does not align with the parameter scalings derived in Theorems 2 and 3. From the analysis, $B = \mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon)$, $T = \mathcal{O}(1 / \epsilon)$, $M = \mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon)$, and $K_c = \mathcal{O}(\log(N/\epsilon))$, leading to $B T = \mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon^2)$ and $M K_c T = \mathcal{O}(N \log(N/\epsilon) / \epsilon^2)$. The dominant term is thus $\mathcal{O}(N \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2)$, not $\mathcal{O}(N^2 \log(N/\epsilon)/\epsilon^2)$. The $N^2$ factor appears unjustified unless an additional source of $N$-dependence—such as consensus variance or mixing time degradation—is explicitly identified. Either correct the bound or provide a derivation showing how the $N^2$ factor arises.

comments.title: Incorrect consensus update in critic step
comments.quote: 11 Consensus Update $w_{k+1}^i \leftarrow \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} A(i,j) \cdot \tilde{w}_k^i$;
comments.explanation: The consensus update in Algorithm 1, line 11, incorrectly applies the weight matrix to the local parameter $\tilde{w}_k^i$ rather than aggregating neighbors' parameters. The correct form should be $w_{k+1}^i \leftarrow \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}} A(i,j) \tilde{w}_k^j$, where the sum is over all agents and each term uses neighbor $j$'s parameter. As written, agent $i$ multiplies its own $\tilde{w}_k^i$ by multiple weights $A(i,j)$, which is mathematically invalid and breaks the averaging dynamics. This error prevents mean preservation and consensus convergence. Correct the update to reflect standard gossip averaging over neighbors' states.

comments.title: Unjustified strict negative definiteness of critic matrix
comments.quote: This property further implies that for all $\theta$, $A_{\pi_{\theta}}$ is invertible and $\lambda_{\max}(A_{\pi_{\theta}}+A_{\pi_{\theta}}^{T})<0$ *(Qiu et al., 2021)*, where $\lambda_{\max}(\cdot)$ is the largest eigenvalues of the matrix.
comments.explanation: The claim that $\lambda_{\max}(A_{\pi_\theta} + A_{\pi_\theta}^T) < 0$ implies strict negative definiteness, but this does not follow from standard average-reward TD analysis. The matrix $A_{\pi_\theta}$ is known to be negative semi-definite on the subspace orthogonal to the stationary distribution, with $\mathbf{1}$ in its null space when rewards are constant. Thus, $\lambda_{\max}(A_{\pi_\theta} + A_{\pi_\theta}^T) \leq 0$, not strictly less than 0. The strict inequality requires additional spectral gap assumptions, which are introduced in Assumption 5. Therefore, attributing strict negativity to Assumption 4 alone is incorrect. Revise the statement to $\leq 0$ and clarify that Assumption 5 strengthens this to $\leq -\lambda_A < 0$.

comments.title: Flawed construction of consensus matrix for non-regular graphs
comments.quote: We remark that for a practical choice of $A$, one can use the following form $A=\frac{1}{\text{deg}(\mathcal{G})}(\text{deg}(\mathcal{G})\cdot I-L)$, where $\text{deg}(\mathcal{G})$ is the degree of the graph $\mathcal{G}$ (i.e. the maximal vertex degree), $I$ is the identity matrix of conforming dimensionality, and $L$ is the Laplacian matrix of the graph. It is easy to verify that this matrix is symmetric, doubly stochastic and $\eta\geq\frac{1}{\text{deg}(\mathcal{G})}\geq\frac{1}{N}$.
comments.explanation: The proposed matrix $A = I - \frac{1}{\text{deg}(\mathcal{G})}L$ fails to satisfy $A_{ii} \geq \eta > 0$ for non-regular graphs. In a star graph with $N$ nodes, the center has degree $N-1 = \text{deg}(\mathcal{G})$, so $A_{ii} = 1 - \frac{N-1}{N-1} = 0$, violating Assumption 3. While symmetry and double stochasticity hold, the uniform positivity condition does not. This undermines the spectral gap guarantees required for consensus convergence. Replace the example with a valid construction such as Metropolis-Hastings weights, which ensure $A_{ij} \geq \frac{1}{N}$ and $A_{ii} \geq \frac{1}{N}$ for all $i,j$.

comments.title: Local TD-error averaging does not yield unbiased advantage estimate
comments.quote: Hence, we have that the expected global TD-error is the advantage function, i.e.,  $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t|s_t, a_t] = A_\theta(s_t, a_t)$ . Thus, we can use this global TD-error as an unbiased estimate of the advantage function.
comments.explanation: The claim that $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t|s_t,a_t] = \mathrm{Adv}_\theta(s_t,a_t)$ relies on $V_\theta(\cdot)$ being the true value function and $\mu_t$ converging to $J(\theta)$. However, $V_\theta(s) = \phi(s)^T w$ is an approximation, and unless $w = w_\theta^*$, the Bellman equation does not hold in expectation. Moreover, $\mu_t$ is a moving average of rewards, not the true $J(\theta)$, so $\mathbb{E}[\bar{r}_{t+1} - \mu_t] \neq J(\theta) - \mu_t$ in finite time. The expectation $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t|s_t,a_t]$ includes both function approximation bias and tracking error from $\mu_t$, so it is not an unbiased estimate of the advantage. The analysis conflates asymptotic convergence with finite-time unbiasedness, which invalidates the gradient estimation guarantee in Theorem 3.

comments.title: Gradient depends on unobservable joint actions
comments.quote: $\nabla_{\theta^{i}}J(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{s\sim d_{\theta},a\sim\pi_{\theta}}[\nabla_{\theta^{i}}\log\pi_{\theta^{i}}^{i}(a^{i}|s)\cdot\text{Adv}_{\theta}(s,a)].$ (4)
comments.explanation: The policy gradient expression requires $a \sim \pi_\theta$, meaning the full joint action $a = (a^1, \dots, a^N)$, and the advantage $\text{Adv}_\theta(s,a)$ depends on this joint action. However, agent $i$ only observes its own action $a^i$ and cannot access $a^{-i}$. Therefore, the expectation cannot be computed locally, making the gradient non-decentralizable as written. A valid decentralized gradient must depend only on locally observable quantities. Either decompose the advantage into local components or assume that the joint action is observable during reward generation. Otherwise, the theorem does not align with the decentralized setting.

comments.title: Incorrect communication bits complexity
comments.quote: The amount of information (in terms of bits) is  $(K_{c}NK + BNt_{\mathrm{gossip}})T = \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^{2}}\log \frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)$ .
comments.explanation: The stated order $\mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$ does not match the expression $(K_c N K + B N t_{\mathrm{gossip}})T$. From the analysis, $K_c = \mathcal{O}(\log(N/\epsilon))$, $K = \mathcal{O}(N/\epsilon)$, $B = \mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon)$, $t_{\mathrm{gossip}} = \mathcal{O}(\log(N/\epsilon))$, and $T = \mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon)$. Substituting, $K_c N K T = \mathcal{O}(\log(N/\epsilon) \cdot N \cdot (N/\epsilon) \cdot (1/\epsilon)) = \mathcal{O}(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$, and $B N t_{\mathrm{gossip}} T = \mathcal{O}((1/\epsilon) \cdot N \cdot \log(N/\epsilon) \cdot (1/\epsilon)) = \mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$. The dominant term is thus $\mathcal{O}(N^2 / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$, not $\mathcal{O}(N / \epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$. Correct the expression to reflect the $N^2$ dependence.

comments.title: Inconsistent state space notation
comments.quote: In this paper, we assume that the global state space  $S$  and action space for agent  $\mathcal{A}^i$  are finite. As a result, the joint action space  $\mathcal{A}$  is also finite for finite  $N$ . We also note that at time  $t \geq 0$ , all agents can observe the current global state  $s_t$ .
comments.explanation: The state space is denoted as $\mathcal{S}$ in Definition 1 but referred to as $S$ in the text. This inconsistency may lead readers to believe $S$ and $\mathcal{S}$ represent different objects. Since no distinction is indicated, this appears to be a typographical error. To maintain clarity, all references to the global state space should use $\mathcal{S}$. Rewrite every instance of $S$ as $\mathcal{S}$.

comments.title: Redundant qualification on joint action finiteness
comments.quote: In this paper, we assume that the global state space  $S$  and action space for agent  $\mathcal{A}^i$  are finite. As a result, the joint action space  $\mathcal{A}$  is also finite for finite  $N$ .
comments.explanation: The phrase 'for finite $N$' is redundant because $N$ is defined as the cardinality of the agent set $\mathcal{N} = \{1,\dots,N\}$, which is inherently finite. The finiteness of $\mathcal{A} = \prod_{i\in\mathcal{N}}\mathcal{A}^i$ follows directly from the finiteness of each $\mathcal{A}^i$ and the finiteness of $\mathcal{N}$. This qualification may misleadingly suggest $N$ could be infinite, which contradicts the setup. Rewrite the sentence to eliminate the redundancy: 'Since each $\mathcal{A}^i$ is finite and $N$ is finite, the joint action space $\mathcal{A}$ is finite.'

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a consensus-based, batch-sampled decentralized actor-critic algorithm for multi-agent reinforcement learning in the average reward setting. The authors derive finite-time convergence rates and sample complexity bounds, claiming an O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)) complexity. The work addresses the challenging lack of joint-action observability by utilizing local TD-error sharing.

The paper provides a significant theoretical contribution by extending finite-time convergence results to the average reward multi-agent setting, which is notoriously difficult. The derivation of sample complexity bounds that match single-agent benchmarks is impressive. However, the reliance on strong assumptions regarding the spectral gap and the lack of clarity on how the communication rounds scale with the network topology require further attention.

Overview issues:

- Dependence on the Spectral Gap and Network Topology: The analysis relies heavily on Assumption 3, which assumes a constant spectral gap for the consensus weight matrix. While this is standard in some literature, the paper does not explicitly characterize how the constants in the convergence rate depend on the topology of the communication graph, such as the algebraic connectivity. Given that the sample complexity is claimed to be O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)), the impact of the network diameter and spectral gap on the hidden constants is critical. The authors should provide a more explicit breakdown of how the mixing time of the communication graph interacts with the sample complexity bound to ensure the result is not overly optimistic for poorly connected networks.

- Ambiguity in the Communication Complexity Definition: The paper claims a communication complexity of O(1/ε log(N/ε)), but the definition of a communication round remains somewhat loose. In Algorithm 2, the gossip process is nested within each outer iteration, and it is unclear if the cost of transmitting the full vector of local parameters is accounted for in the final complexity claim. If the communication cost grows with the dimension of the policy parameter space or the number of agents, this should be explicitly reflected in the complexity notation. I suggest the authors clarify the total bit-complexity of the communication rounds to ensure it is comparable to existing decentralized MARL literature.

- Handling of the Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Critic Estimation: In Section 5.1, the critic learning process uses a constant step-size β. While the authors argue that this avoids the non-vanishing error found in Qiu et al. (2021), the proof relies on a specific choice of batch size M and iteration number Kc. The paper does not sufficiently discuss the sensitivity of the convergence to the choice of β relative to the mixing time of the underlying Markov chain. If the Markov chain has a slow mixing time, the bias induced by the Markovian sampling could dominate the error term. It would be beneficial to add a discussion on how the mixing time of the state-action process affects the required batch size M to maintain the O(ε^-2) rate.

- Lack of Explicit Dependence on State-Action Space Size: The complexity bound is stated as O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)), but the dependence on the state space |S| and action space |A| is suppressed within the big-O notation. In many MARL settings, the state-action space grows exponentially with the number of agents N. If the constants hidden in the big-O notation scale poorly with |S| or |A|, the claim of scalability is weakened. The authors should explicitly state the dependence of their bounds on the cardinality of the state and action spaces to provide a more honest assessment of the algorithm's scalability.

- Need for a concrete, fully-specified numerical example: The paper provides a theoretical convergence rate but lacks a concrete, fully-specified example to calibrate the reader's intuition on the constants. While the paper includes experimental results, these are empirical comparisons rather than a verification of the theoretical bounds in a controlled, known-ground-truth setting. I suggest adding a simple, small-scale example, such as a multi-agent grid world or a coordination game with a known optimal policy and transition matrix, where the authors can compute the theoretical constants and demonstrate that the empirical convergence rate aligns with the derived O(epsilon^-2) complexity.

- Missing discussion on the impact of the mixing time on sample complexity: The sample complexity bound is derived under the assumption of a stationary distribution, but the dependence on the mixing time of the underlying Markov chain is largely suppressed. In the average reward setting, the mixing time is a primary driver of the error in TD-learning. The paper needs to explicitly discuss how the mixing time of the state-action chain affects the choice of the batch size M and the overall sample complexity. Providing a specific bound that incorporates the spectral gap of the transition matrix P would significantly strengthen the paper's claims regarding the algorithm's robustness.

- Gap between theoretical convergence and practical implementation: The algorithm requires specific choices for the batch sizes M and B, as well as the gossip iterations t_gossip, which are functions of epsilon and the spectral properties of the communication graph. These requirements are difficult to satisfy in practice without prior knowledge of the system's global parameters. The paper would benefit from a practical implementation guide or a discussion on how agents can adaptively tune these parameters in a decentralized manner to maintain the claimed convergence rate without needing global knowledge of the network's spectral gap.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Explicitly derive the dependence of the convergence constants on the spectral gap of the communication graph.

- Clarify the total bit-complexity of the communication rounds in Algorithm 2 to ensure it is clearly distinguished from the number of gossip rounds.

- Add a discussion or a small numerical sensitivity analysis regarding the impact of the Markov chain mixing time on the critic estimation error.

- Explicitly state the dependence of the sample complexity on the state-action space size |S| and |A|.

comments.title: Bias in Local TD-Error Definition
comments.quote: tion. For agent  $i$ , the local TD-error can be computed as  $\delta_t^i = r_{t+1}^i - \mu_t^i + V_\theta(s_{t+1}) - V_\theta(s_t)$ , where  $\mu_{t+1}^i = (1 - \alpha)\mu_t^i + \alpha r_{
comments.explanation: The local TD-error delta_t^i is biased because it subtracts the local average reward estimate mu_t^i rather than the global average reward J(theta). Since mu_t^i converges to the local average reward J^i(theta), the expectation E[delta_t^i] does not equal the advantage function. The gossip step in Algorithm 2 averages the TD-errors, but it does not correct the bias introduced by the local mu_t^i. Please include a consensus update for mu_t^i to ensure agents converge to the global average reward.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Sample Complexity Reporting
comments.quote: le complexity. We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is $\mathcal{O}(N^{2}/\epsilon^{2}\log(N/\epsilon))$. Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actor-critic algorithms for reinforcement
comments.explanation: The claim that your complexity matches single-agent actor-critic algorithms is misleading because the single-agent bound lacks the N^2 factor. If this factor is necessary for multi-agent coordination, it represents a significant overhead. Please clarify that the N^2 term is an additional cost arising from the decentralized setting and avoid suggesting the bounds are identical.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Communication Complexity Scaling
comments.quote: $(K_{c}NK + BNt_{\mathrm{gossip}})T = \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{N}{\epsilon^{2}}\log \frac{N}{\epsilon}\right)$
comments.explanation: The provided expression appears algebraically inconsistent with the scaling of its components. Given K_c = O(log(1/epsilon)), B = O(1/epsilon), and T = O(1/epsilon^2), the term B * N * t_gossip * T should scale as O(N/epsilon^3 log(1/epsilon)).
